coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence device for in-situ and accurate determination of CA153 at the MCF-7 cell surface based on graphene quantum dots loaded surface villous Au nanocage
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Fang
Ge, Shenguang
Su, Min
Song, Xianrang
Yan, Mei
Yu, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: In our work, low potential and sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) detection for CA153 on MCF-7 cell surface was firstly achieved based on a microfluidic paper-based analytical device. Au nanoflowers were grown in paper working electrode (PWE) to construct the ECL platform for primary antibodies immobilizing. After MCF-7 cells were captured in the modified PWE, and subsequently recognized with ECL signal substance labeled secondary antibodies, the sandwich-type cell sensor was fabricated. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) loaded surface villous Au nanocages were synthesized as the ECL signal substance, and characterized by scanning electron microscope. The specific villous surface structure for Au nanocage permitted more GQDs being carried and electrons transporting, which largely benefited for the ECL performance. As the un-negligible impendence for MCF-7 cell would definitely influence the ECL signal, CA153 labeled HMEpic cell was used as the control cell to obtain more accurate determination result. This low-cost and fast strategy showed acceptable biocompatibility and stability, and will be promising for cellular immunochemistry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CA153
Electrochemiluminescence
Surface villous Au nanocages
Au nanoflowers
Paper working electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of PWE with Au nanoflowers grown in it
Preparation of the composite of GQDs@SVAu nanocage labeled Ab2
Preparation of CA153 labeled HMEpic cell
Characterizations of PWE with Au nanoflowers grown in it
Characterizations of GQDs and SVAu nanocage
Possible mechanism for the ECL behaviors of the GQDs@SVAu composite
Characterization of the improved ECL performance based on µ-PAD modified with Au nanoflowers and other Au nanoparticles
EIS Characterization of the cell biosensor
Analytical performance of CA153 on MCF-7 cancer cell
Cytotoxicity, specificity and stability of our ECL cell assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the as-prepared Au nanoparticle seeds (A); SEM images of bare paper sample zone of PWE (B); Growth of Au nanoflowers layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in paper sample zone of PWE under different magnification: (C, D, E); EDS of Au nanoflowers modified paper sample zone (F).
TEM image of the GQDs. (Inset: size distributions of GQDs) (A); SEM of cubic Cu2O (B); SEM of SVAu nanocage (C); PL spectra of GQDs and GQDs@SVAu nanocage (D).
ECL-potential curves of pure GQDs (a) and GQDs@SVAu nanocages (b) (A);. EIS of the different modified PWEs in 5.0 mmolL− 1 Fe(CN)6 4−/3− containing 0.1molL− 1 KCl (B).

ECL profiles of the ECL cell sensor in the presence of different concentrations of CA153 used to modify normal cells in PBS containing 0.05M K2S2O8 and 0.1M KCl. Inset: calibration curve for CA153 determination. CA153 determination (UmL−1): 0 (a), 0.005 (b), 0.01 (c), 0.1 (d), 1.0 (e) , 10 (f), 100 (g), 500 (h) (A); ECL profiles of cell sensor for MCF-7 cancer cells and normal HMEpic cells (B).
Fluorescent evaluation of Au nanoflower-PWE cytotoxicity after varied incubation time with MCF-7 cancer cells (A); ECL values of different cells (B).

Schematic representation of the fabrication procedures for this cell semsor. Bare PCE: (a) wax-penetrated paper, (b) cellulose fibers in the paper cell zone (unprinted macroporous paper), (c) screen-printed carbon working electrode (A); Growth of Au nanoflowers layer on cellulose fiber surfaces (B); Immobilization of Ab1, blocking with BSA and capture of MCF-7 cell (C); Immobilization of Ab2/GQDs@SVAu bioconjugates (D).

head.ce:title: Electrochemiluminescence device for in-situ and accurate determination of CA153 at the MCF-7 cell surface based on graphene quantum dots loaded surface villous Au nanocage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cancer Research Center, Shandong Tumor Hospital, Jinan 250117, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cancer Research Center, Shandong Tumor Hospital

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In our work, low potential and sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) detection for CA153 on MCF-7 cell surface was firstly achieved based on a microfluidic paper-based analytical device. Au nanoflowers were grown in paper working electrode (PWE) to construct the ECL platform for primary antibodies immobilizing. After MCF-7 cells were captured in the modified PWE, and subsequently recognized with ECL signal substance labeled secondary antibodies, the sandwich-type cell sensor was fabricated. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) loaded surface villous Au nanocages were synthesized as the ECL signal substance, and characterized by scanning electron microscope. The specific villous surface structure for Au nanocage permitted more GQDs being carried and electrons transporting, which largely benefited for the ECL performance. As the un-negligible impendence for MCF-7 cell would definitely influence the ECL signal, CA153 labeled HMEpic cell was used as the control cell to obtain more accurate determination result. This low-cost and fast strategy showed acceptable biocompatibility and stability, and will be promising for cellular immunochemistry.

ce:section.ce:para: Paper, one of the most important inventions in the human civilization and used for centuries, was endowed with transformative applications in 2007 in the innovative studies of Whitesides's group to construct microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (μ-PADs or Lab-on-paper) (Martinez et al. 2007) using patterned paper substrate as microfluidic platform with the reduced dependence on expensive external instrumentation (e.g. no pumps are needed). The most obvious benefits are the low-cost of paper and the highly developed infrastructure of the printing industry, making production of μ-PADs both economical and scalable. Our group has developed a series of analytical applications on μ-PADs for the sensitive detection of small molecules, proteins, and DNAs (Derda et al., 2009; Ge et al., 2013a, 2013b, 2012a; Liu et al., 2014a). Recently, based on the virtues of flexibility, disposability, great biodegradability and chemo/bio-compatibility, paper has been exploited as a novel platform for cytological or histological studies. And the emerging paper-based cyto-devices (Deiss et al., 2014; Deiss et al., 2013; Derda et al., 2009, 2011; Li et al., 2011; Qi et al., 2014) offered a promising potential to realize in-situ, simple, miniaturized, cytological/histological researches in various area for basic science advancement, clinical diagnostics and therapeutics.

Tumor biomarkers are substances that can be found in the body when cancer is present and whose identification and measurement holds significant promise for the early detection of cancer and therapy monitoring (Ge et al., 2012b; Song et al., 2011). The expression patterns of surface antigens on cells are related to various diseases. The advent of rapid and simple detection strategies for surface antigens has provided great expectations for the development of a useful tool for early medical diagnosis and prognosis of various diseases (Campana and Behm, 2000). Thus, sensitive, precise, and accurate assays for measuring surface antigens on cells will be valuable tools in a wide range of clinical applications. There has been considerable efforts in recent years to develop immunoassays including fluorescence immunoassay (Copple et al., 2012) and electrochemical immunoassay (Wen et al., 2011). Compared with these immunoassays, the development of immunoassays based on other detection strategies such as electrochemiluminescence (ECL) are rare. ECL, which has been widely applied in bioanalytical applications, combines the analytical advantages of chemiluminescent analysis with electrochemical assay such as low background, specificity of ECL reaction, simple instrumentation, and low cost.

Recently, various kinds of the ECL reagents have been developed. Highly luminescent semiconductor nanocrystals (NCs), often referred to quantum dots (QDs), have generated much excitement for a wide variety of promising applications in optoelectronic devices, biological labeling and biomedicine. Some NCs, such as CdS (Myung et al., 2004), CdTe (Liu et al., 2012) and CdSe/ZnSe (Myung et al., 2003), can be used as ECL regents in bioanalytical applications (Jie et al., 2009; Liu et al., 2013; Shan et al., 2010). Although these semiconductor QDs have been accepted as ECL indicators for their effective and valuable ECL behavior, one challenge regarding these heavy metals have raised serious health and environmental concerns (Shan et al., 2010). Therefore, searching for benign nanomaterials with good ECL activities has become an urgent challenge.

Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms densely packed in honey comb two-dimensional (2D) lattice, has extensive applications in physics, chemistry and material science, owing to its unique electronic, thermal, mechanical and chemical properties (Xing et al., 2012). 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as recently emerging carbon-based materials, are graphene sheets smaller than 100nm (Ponomarenko et al., 2008). They exhibit new phenomena due to quantum confinement and edge effects. GQDs are attracting considerable attention as they may gradually replace traditional semiconductor QDs due to their superiority in chemical inertness, low toxicity biocompatibility, high fluorescent activity and excellent photostability (Liu et al., 2014b; Shen et al., 2012). Recently, to further improve the ECL performance of QDs, a lot of nanomaterials, such as SiO2, graphene, and carbon nanotube, have been introduced to load more QDs (Chen et al., 2012; Qian et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2009). And here surface villous Au nanocage (SVAu nanocage) was applied as carrier for GQDs loading on to obtain higher ECL intensity and lower applied potential. This can be explained that SVAu nanocage showed large specific surface area and excellent electronic transmission efficiency. The composite of GQDs and SVAu nanocage (GQDs@SVAu nanocage) was used to label signal antibodies (Ab2).

To improve the ECL platform, Au nanoflowers were grown on the surface of paper fiber in paper working electrode (PWE). The introduction of Au nanoflowers could largely enhance the ECL performance due to the good biocompatibility and large specific surface for immobilizing antibodies and remarkable conductivity for promoting ECL signal. After the primary antibodies (Ab1) were fixed on Au nanoflowers, the cancer cells (using MCF-7 cancer cell as the model cell) were specifically immobilized on Au nanoflowers through the specific binding of carbohydrate antigen 153 (CA153) on MCF-7 cells with CA153-Ab1 ( Scheme 1). Then GQDs@SVAu nanocage labeled Ab2 were applied to identify CA-153 on the cell surface to produce strong ECL signal. And CA153 labeled normal HMEpic cells were used as standard cells to obtain more accurate results. By coupling the composite of GQDs@SVAu nanocage with Au nanoflowers modified paper electrode, the present ECL cell sensor showed an excellent analytical performance for the sensitive detection of CA153 on cells.

All chemicals and solvents used here were aseptic after heat sterilization treatment. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and CA153, CA153-Ab1, CA153-Ab2 were provided from Shanghai Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). The human breast cancer cells MCF-7 and human epithelial breast cell line 7610 (HMEpic) were provided by Shandong Tumor Hospital.

0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) was employed as the supporting electrolyte. Carbon ink (ED423ss) and Ag/AgCl ink (CNC-01) were purchased from Acheson. Whatman chromatography paper #1 (58.0cm×68.0cm) (pure cellulose paper) was obtained from GE Healthcare Worldwide (Pudong, Shanghai, China) and used with further adjustment of size (A4 size). Graphite powder (K S-10, 99.95%), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), glutaraldehyde (GA), N-(3-dimethylamminopropyl)- N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and L-cysteine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

The ECL experiments were performed on MPI-B ECL analyzer (Remax, Xi'an) equipped with a photomultiplier (PMT) and a potentiostat. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a RF-5301 spectrofluorometer (P.C. Shimadzu, Japan). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) analyses were performed using QUANTA FEG 250 thermal field emission scanning electron microscopy (FEI Co., USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from a JEOL JEM-1400 microscope (JEOL, Japan). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured on a CHI 119 760D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua). Ultrasonic processing was performed on a JY92-IIDN ultrasonic apparatus (Ningbo Scientz biotechnology Co. Ltd., China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PWE with Au nanoflowers grown in it
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the growth of Au nanoflowers, Au naoparticles were prepared according to a facile method described as follows. HAuCl4 and trisodium citrate solutions were filtered through a 0.22µm microporous membrane filter prior to use, and then 3.0mL of 1% trisodium citrate was added to 20mL of boiling 0.05% HAuCl4 solution and stirred for 30min at the boiling point. The final Au naoparticles prepared by this method have an average diameter of ∼13nm.

Prior to the Ab1 immobilization, growth of Au nanoflowers layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in the paper working zone was implemented to fabricate a novel Au nanoflowers-PWE with enlarged effective surface area and enhanced electron transport property for ECL. This novel Au nanoflowers-PWE was fabricated on this µ-PAD through a direct chemical reduction of HAuCl4 by AA. Fist, as-prepared Au nanoparticle seeds solution (15.0µL) was dropped into the bare PWE. Then the origami device was equilibrated at room temperature for 1h to optimize the surface immobilization of Au nanoparticle seeds on cellulose fibers. After rinsing with water thoroughly to remove loosely bound Au nanoparticle seeds, freshly prepared Au-growth solution (15.0µL) containing 0.50mM HAuCl4 and 0.25mM AA was applied into the Au nanoparticles seeded PWE, respectively, and incubated at room temperature for 4min. During the growth process, the Au nanoparticle seeds acted as catalysts for the reduction of HAuCl4 by AA, resulting in the enhanced growth of the Au nanoflowers. Subsequently, the resulting Au nanoflowers-PWEs were washed with water thoroughly. Thus a layer of interconnected Au nanoflowers on cellulose fibers with good conductivity was obtained (Scheme 1B), which was dried at room temperature for 20min.

The preparation of GQDs was similar to a previous protocol (Zhu et al., 2011) with slight modifications and the procedure was described below. Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers method (Hummers and Offeman, 1958). Briefly, GO was dissolved in dimethyl formamide (DMF) with the concentrations of 270mgmL−1. The GO/DMF solutions were under ultrasonication for 30 min. Subsequently, the GO/DMF solutions were transferred to Teflon-sealed autoclave, maintained at 180°C for 6h and then quenched by lowering the temperature naturally. The resulting product was purified by evaporating the brown transparent suspension and redispersed in water.

Firstly, Cu2O precursor templates were synthesized. 2mL of mixed solution, composed of 0.74M sodium citrate and 0.2M sodium carbonate, was added to 18mL solution of CuSO4 (0.038M) dropwise. After that, 3.2g PVP was added and stirred vigorously until PVP was dissolved completely. Then, the mixed solution in the flask mentioned above was aged in a water bath at 90°C for 2.5h. Cu2O nanoparticles with different morphology could be prepared in the same synthetic system by simply changing the PVP component amount from 2.9g to 5.2g. Different amounts of the PVP template could generate various product shapes. The brick-red products were collected and rinsed thoroughly before being dried under vacuum.

The Cu2O cubic precursors were dispersed in 8mL of PVP (0.5%) aqueous solution and then 12mL distilled water was added, followed by adding 2.4mL of HAuCl4 (10mM) solution under vigorous stir at room temperature. After 6h, the black precipitate was collected and dispersed in 40mL solution containing 0.04% PVP and 1% ammonia for about 12h. The precipitate was then separated and re-dispersed in 40mL solution containing 0.04% PVP and 1% ammonia again for another 12h. At last, the SVAu nanocage was obtained by centrifugation, washing with distilled water and drying under vacuum overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the composite of GQDs@SVAu nanocage labeled Ab2
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of GQDs@ SVAu nanocage, 30mgmL−1 of SVAu nanocage were mixed with 20mgmL−1 L-cysteine for 1h under sonication. After washing, SVAu nanocage could be modified with animo through the Au–S bond. At the same time, –COOH activated GQDs were prepared by mixing GQDs with 1.0mL EDC (20mgmL−1). Then, the resulting amino-functionalized SVAu nanocage was dispersed in 1.0mL of –COOH activated GQDs. The mixed suspension was stirred at 4°C for 2h to obtain GQDs@SVAu nanocage in the presence of –COOH on GQDs. Finally, the product was thoroughly rinsed with water, and dried in 60°C for 12h to obtain GQDs@SVAu nanocages. Then, 1.0mg of the composite was immediately redispersed into a mixture of 1mL PBS solution (pH 7.4, 10mM) containing EDC (20mgmL−1) and Ab2 (20μgmL−1), followed by incubation at room temperature for 50min to form Ab2/GQDs@SVAu bioconjugates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CA153 labeled HMEpic cell
ce:section.ce:para: The MCF-7 cancer cells, with a diameter of about 12μm, may cause the largely decrease in the ECL signal due to the existing significant impedence. So different concentrations of CA153 labeled normal HMEpic cells were chosen as the standard cells. Firstly, CA153 standard solutions with different concentration were prepared. Then, 1mL of HMEpic cells (1.0×106 cellsmL−1) were incubated with 1mL of the various CA153 concentrations: 0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10, 100, 500 (UmL−1) with the addition of GA as a linker for animo.

A sandwich-type cell sensing platform was constructed on the PWE of our μ-PADs, as shown in Scheme 1. The Au nanoflowers modified PWE was incubated with Ab1 (5μL, 20μgmL− 1) for 20min, followed by washing with PBS buffer to remove unspecific physically adsorption. Then the modified PWE was soaked in 1% BSA solution at 4°C for 20min to block the nonspecific binding sites on the electrode surface. The as-prepared biosensors (designed as BSA/Ab1/Au-PWEs) were used for detection of CA153 on cell surface.

To carry out the immunoreaction and ECL detections, the 3D origami ECL cyto-device was firstly incubated with a 10.0μL of sample solution containing different concentrations of CA153 labeled normal HMEpic cells in PBS for 20min at room temperature. After washing with PBS buffer, the origami ECL cyto-device was then incubated with 10.0μL of Ab2/GQDs@SVAu bioconjugates for 20min at room temperature, followed by washing with PBS buffer. Then, 10.0μL of PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.05M K2S2O8 was added. Cyclic voltammetry measurements were used to trigger the ECL reaction in the sweeping range from 0 to −1.35V with a scan rate of 100mVs−1 at room temperature. The ECL emission was detected by the PMT biased at 800V. The ECL signals related to CA153 concentrations could be detected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of PWE with Au nanoflowers grown in it
ce:section.ce:para: The PWE was modified through the growth of a Au nanoflowers layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in paper sample zone from Au nanoparticle seeds to fabricate this novel Au nanoflowers modified PWE. Fig. 1A showed the TEM image of the as-prepared Au nanoparticle seeds with the average diameter of about 13nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the porous bare paper sample possessed high ratio of surface area to weight with rough cellulose fibers, which could offer an excellent adsorption microenvironment for the Au nanoparticles. After Au nanoparticles were successfully seeded and grown on the cellulose fiber, a continuous and dense Au nanoflowers conducting layer on the cellulose fiber surfaces was observed (Fig. 1C, D ). The enlarged image of Au nanoflowers modified cellulose fiber was shown in Fig. 1E, showing the uniform growth of Au nanoflowers. This indicated that a good coverage of Au nanoflowers on the surfaces of the cellulose fibers was obtained using our simple growth method. The Au nanoflowers modified paper sample zone maintained good 3D interwoven and incompact cellulose fibers networks structure after the growth process, which would facilitate the area-to-volume ratio as well as increase the catalytic active sites to obtain better electron transmission performance. In addition, the successful growth of Au nanoflowers on cellulose fibers was confirmed by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) (Fig. 1F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of GQDs and SVAu nanocage
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2A was the TEM image of GQDs, showing a uniform distribution. The size of GQDs was mostly at 7nm from the inset of Fig. 2A. Typical SEM images of the as-prepared Cu2O cubic and Au cubic cages were given in Fig. 2B and C. Fig. 2B indicated that the Cu2O cubic consists purely of well-defined cubic with a diameter distribution of about 400nm. The size of the Au nanocages agreed with that of Cu2O templates, and showed a special villous surface. Some crushed products in Fig. 2C announced that these cubic had big hollow interiors. Fig. 2D showed PL spectra of GQDs and GQDs@SVAu nanocage. A red shift was occurred to GQDs@SVAu nanocage compared with pure GQDs, confirming the successful preparation of GQDs@SVAu nanocage. The mechanism for the red shift of the PL spectra can be explained from the Stokes shift. Concretely, the distance between GQDs decreased, so the dipolar interaction enhanced, resulting in the increase of Stokes shift, and finally the red shift occurred (Gao et al., 2012; Shan et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the ECL behaviors of the GQDs@SVAu composite
ce:section.ce:para:
Obviously, the ECL behaviors of both GQDs are similar to that of other QDs, such as CdSe (Li et al., 2012), carbon NCs (Dong et al., 2010), and Si NCs (Ding et al., 2002). Consequently, the ECL mechanism of the GQDs was proposed as follows: firstly, strongly oxidizing
SO
4
• -
radicals and
GQDs
• -
radicals were produced by electrochemical reduction of
S
2
O
8
2 -
and GQDs, respectively. Then,
SO
4
• -
radicals could react with
GQDs
• -
via electron-transfer annihilation, producing an excited state (
GQDs
*
) that finally emitted light. The possible ECL mechanisms are described as following equations:
(1)
GQDs + n
e
-
→
GQDs
• -
(2)
S
2
O
8
2 -
+
e
-
→
SO
4
2 -
+
SO
4
• -
(3)
GQDs
• -
+
SO
4
• -
→
GQDs
*
+
SO 4
2 -

(4)
GQDs
*
→ GQDs + h ν
In order to identify the superior ECL performance of GQDs@SVAu nanocage, ECL-potential curves for the GQDs@SVAu nanocage constructed sandwich-type cell sensor were measured, as well as those of pure GQDs. As shown in Fig.3A, compared with pure GQDs, it can be seen that the ECL intensity of GQDs@SVAu nanocage was about 5-fold greater than that of pure GQDs. And the ECL peak voltage (−1.35V) was much more positive than that of pure GQDs (−1.45V) as well as the ECL onset voltage. These phenomena indicated that the presence of SVAu nanocage obviously increased the load of GQDs and decreased the potential barriers (Chen et al., 2014). On the other hand, SVAu nanocage with high conductivity will induce more SO4 ·− improving the quantity of the transmission state (GQDs*), which can also contribute to the enhanced ECL intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the improved ECL performance based on µ-PAD modified with Au nanoflowers and other Au nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The modified µ-PADs were scanned from 0V to −1.35V with scan rate of 100mVs−1 to collect ECL signals. To investigate the amplification technique of the Au nanoflowers for ECL analysis, control experiments with the substitution of common Au nanoparticles (Fig. S1A) were carried out and the results were shown in Fig. S1B. We compared the ECL intensity of different cell sensors with varied CA153 concentrations based on µ-PAD modified with Au nanoflowers (curve a) and common Au nanoparticles (curve b). The quantity of the GQDs@SVAu nanocage was equal in both experiments. As can be observed that the cell sensors based on µ-PAD modified with Au nanoflowers revealed excellent ECL performance compared with common Au nanoparticles. And this demonstrated the superior performance in our ECL cell sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS Characterization of the cell biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: As an effective tool for characterizing the interface properties of electrodes, EIS was applied to probe the cell sensor fabrication process using K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 as a redox probe. Fig. 3B displayed the Nyquist diagrams of each PWE involving different construction step of the cell sensor. The probe showed a low electron-transfer resistance (R et) at a bare PWE (curve a). After Au nanoflowers were grown in it, R et decreased to a large extent (curve b), due to the high electrical conductivity of Au nanoflowers. Subsequently, Ab1 and BSA (curve c and d) were modified on the surface of Au nanoflowers, resulting in the gradually increase of R et. Then, MCF-7 breast cancer cells (curve e) were constructed, and the R et increased largely. Finally, when Ab2/GQDs@SVAu bioconjugates were added into the modified PWE, the Ret increased slightly. The elevating hindrance effect and insulating effect introduced by biological materials suggested successful stepwise fabrication of the proposed cell sensor.

Under optimized conditions, the analytical performance for CA153 on cancer cells by using this method was verified by applying various concentrations of CA153 labeled HMEpic cell in PBS. With the increasing concentrations of CA153, good correlation between the logarithm concentration of CA153 and the ECL peak intensity with a wide dynamic range (0.005–500UmL−1) was also observed ( Fig. 4A). The linear regression equation was ECL=5741.20+2277.64 lgcCA153 (UmL− 1), with a correlation coefficient of 0.99874. The limit of detection at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 for CA153 was 0.0014UmL−1. For comparison, the analytical properties of the fabricated biosensor were compared with the previously reported CA153 biosensors based on utilization of different materials and methods (Table S1). These results adequately suggested that our cell assay was advantageous in sensitivity and working range. The greatly improved performance for our immunoassay can be attributed to the excellent electrical properties and specific surface area of Au nanoflowers and SVAu nanocages used in our ECL assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of CA153 on MCF-7 cancer cell
ce:section.ce:para: Information on the concentration of CA153 on MCF-7 cancer cells can be inferred from the ECL response. We did the CA153 determination at a concentration of 1.0×106 cellsmL−1 for MCF-7 cells, instead of CA153 labeled normal HMEpic cells. As shown in Fig. 4B, the ECL signal for the MCF-7 cells (4370a.u.) was much higher than that of the separated HMEpic cells (511a.u.), demonstrating a mass of existence of CA153 on MCF-7 cancer cell. As a result, the concentration of CA153 for 1.0×106 cellsmL−1 of MCF-7 cells were calculated to be 0.25UmL−1 through the linear regression equation, and finally 0.25µU CA153 for single MCF-7 cell was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity, specificity and stability of our ECL cell assay
ce:section.ce:para: To provide the information on the cytotoxicity of the Au nanoflowers-PWE, we chose to assess MCF-7 cells through fluorescent imaging technique in this Au nanoflowers-PWE. Calcein AM was used as the fluorescent bioprobes to characterize the cell viability. For the viable cell screening, the captured MCF-7 cells (1.0×106 cellsmL− 1, 10μL) were cultured in-situ in the four different BSA/Ab1/Au-PWEs for 1h, 4h, 7h, 10h and 12h, respectively, Then, MCF-7 cells were incubated in-situ with 0.5μM calcein AM solution at 37°C for 20min. The fluorescent intensity illustrated the amount of viable MCF-7 cells. The result of the fluorescent imaging technique was shown in Fig. 5A, including the graphical versions of the fluorescent intensities. It can be observed that fluorescent intensities increased gradually, with the incubation time varying from 1h to 12h. This result demonstrated that our Au nanoflower-PWE showed no obviously diminishing of the cell viability, due to the good biocompatibility of the grown Au nanoflowers and cellulose fibers.

The specificity of the sensor for MCF-7 was evaluated under optimal detection conditions. Contrast experiments were carried out using 1.0×106 cellsmL−1 of the human chronic myelogenous leukemia cell line (K-562) and the lung carcinoma cell line (A-549), which shows no presence of CA153. As shown in Fig. 5B, a strong ECL signal was observed for MCF-7 cells; however, other cells did not give rise to ECL signal increase, compared with the blank ones. The results showed that the proposed method has good specificity for the breast cancer cells.

The stability of the GQDs@SVAu nanocages stored at room temperature was 96.3% in the first month and then decreased by 8.6% in the next 2 months. GQDs@SVAu nanocages stored in the refrigerator showed no decrease in response after 3 months. These demonstrated that the GQDs@SVAu nanocages in wide temperature ranges are robust.

In this paper, a low potential resolved ECL assay was achieved for the detection of CA153 antigens at the cell surface based on Au nanoflower-PWE and GQDs@SVAu nanocage. The former was used to immobilize more Ab1 with large specific surface area and good conductivity, resulting in the greatly enhanced ECL intensity. The latter was applied to label Ab2, and more GQDs were loaded due to the villous surface and nanocage structure of SVAu nanoparticles, bringing the improved ECL performance. The introduction of excellent ECL probe permitted the strategy to analyze surface antigens during low potential scanning. And the accurate result was obtained by using CA153 labeled MCF-7 cells as the standard cells. Low-cost, fast, in-situ, and sensitive detection for CA153 on MCF-7 cell surface was achieved by using our strategy based on μ-PADs. Also, the success of this method will provide an alternative technology for the cellular immunochemistry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475052, 21175058, 21277058); Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2012BZ002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.04.051.
